refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) does not exceed the customer-defined E_target over the agreed evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across the range of supported worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 is a key performance indicator (KPI) requirement that must be decomposed into measurable, controllable functional elements. The refined statement clarifies that energy efficiency is a system-level outcome dependent on multiple subsystems (propulsion, hydraulics, teleoperation overhead, safety services overhead) and operational factors. It establishes the evaluation boundary (agreed period, nominal conditions, supported scenarios) to enable testability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Establish baseline energy consumption and payload metrics across representative worksite scenarios. Measure total energy input (fuel, electrical) and total mass moved (ton-km or equivalent). Calculate energy_per_ton for each scenario. Aggregate over evaluation period and compare against E_target. Decompose variance to identify which subsystems or operational modes drive inefficiency.
sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The propulsion subsystem shall minimize energy consumption per unit distance traveled while maintaining required tractive effort and speed.
FR-EE-002: The hydraulic subsystem shall minimize energy losses in power transmission and actuation while maintaining required force and flow rates.
FR-EE-003: The teleoperation subsystem shall minimize energy overhead (communication, processing, latency compensation) relative to autonomous or direct-control modes.
FR-EE-004: The safety services subsystem shall minimize energy overhead for monitoring, alerting, and intervention while maintaining required safety integrity.
FR-EE-005: The system shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility and efficiency metrics to operators and site managers to enable operational optimization.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized energy management system that monitors, predicts, and optimizes energy consumption across all subsystems via closed-loop feedback control and operator guidance.

DP-B: Decompose energy efficiency into subsystem-level targets (propulsion efficiency, hydraulic efficiency, teleoperation overhead budget, safety overhead budget) and allocate design margins; implement independent optimization within each subsystem.

DP-C: Implement a hybrid approach: establish system-level energy budget and KPI tracking (DP-A foundation), allocate subsystem targets (DP-B discipline), and integrate real-time telemetry feedback to guide operator behavior and system reconfiguration.
DP-D: Implement energy efficiency through hardware-only optimization (engine efficiency, hydraulic component selection, lightweight materials) without active monitoring or control feedback.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Implement a hybrid energy management architecture comprising: (1) system-level energy budget allocation and KPI tracking via centralized monitoring; (2) subsystem-level efficiency targets with design margins; (3) real-time telemetry feedback (energy consumption, efficiency metrics) to operators and site managers; (4) operator guidance and system reconfiguration recommendations to optimize energy_per_ton within operational constraints.

potential_sources: Coupling between propulsion and hydraulic subsystems: engine load and fuel consumption depend on hydraulic demand; optimization of one affects the other. Mitigation: allocate targets based on typical duty cycles and validate via integrated testing.

Coupling between teleoperation overhead and safety services: both consume communication bandwidth and processing resources. Mitigation: establish separate energy budgets and monitor cross-subsystem interactions.

Coupling between operator behavior and system efficiency: operator decisions (speed, load, idle time) dominate energy consumption. Mitigation: provide real-time feedback and guidance; design DP to enable operator optimization rather than mandate it.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is selected because it acknowledges these couplings explicitly through integrated monitoring and feedback, rather than assuming independence (DP-B risk) or relying solely on hardware optimization (DP-D risk). DP-A alone risks over-automation and loss of operator situational awareness.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Telemetry infrastructure (fuel flow sensors, power meters, GPS, CAN bus) is available and accurate to ±5% across all operating conditions. Risk: Sensor drift or failure could invalidate KPI measurement. Mitigation: implement sensor validation and redundancy.

Assumption: Operators will respond to real-time efficiency feedback and guidance. Risk: Operator behavior may not change if guidance conflicts with productivity or comfort. Mitigation: design guidance to align with operator incentives; validate via field trials.

Assumption: Subsystem-level efficiency targets are achievable without major redesign. Risk: Targets may be infeasible given current hardware or operational constraints. Mitigation: conduct feasibility study during detailed design; establish contingency targets.

Assumption: Energy overhead of teleoperation and safety services is quantifiable and separable. Risk: Overhead may be non-linear or context-dependent. Mitigation: characterize overhead empirically across representative scenarios.

Risk (High): Safety criticality of energy management—if energy monitoring or guidance fails, operator may lose situational awareness or make unsafe decisions. Mitigation: ensure energy feedback is advisory only; maintain independent safety monitoring.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly separate the energy management logic (monitoring, prediction, guidance) from safety-critical functions. Establish clear failure modes and fallback behavior: if energy monitoring fails, system shall revert to safe default operation without energy optimization. Define the interface between energy management and safety services to prevent coupling of non-safety and safety-critical logic.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: System-level energy management, KPI tracking, and operator guidance. Owns the integration of energy telemetry from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. Responsible for energy budget allocation, real-time efficiency metrics computation, and operator interface for energy feedback and guidance.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives energy consumption data (fuel flow, electrical power, engine load, hydraulic pressure/flow, GPS position, vehicle speed) from Vehicle subsystem. Receives communication overhead metrics from Teleoperation subsystem. Receives safety service activation events and energy overhead from Safety Services subsystem.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational tasks and constraints from Site Manager / Equipment Operator (via Teleoperation or direct interface). Sends energy-optimized task recommendations and operator guidance (e.g., suggested speed, load distribution, idle reduction) back to operator.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety-critical alerts and intervention events from Safety Services subsystem to ensure energy management does not compromise safety. Energy guidance shall not override safety constraints.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates energy metrics and KPI status to Site Manager dashboard and Maintenance/Quality systems for fleet-level energy performance tracking and predictive maintenance.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level integration point and owns the energy KPI requirement. Energy management logic (monitoring, prediction, guidance) is naturally contained here as a cross-cutting concern. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry aggregation and interface adaptation required in Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services.

Vehicle subsystem owns propulsion and hydraulic efficiency (FR-EE-001, FR-EE-002) but does not own system-level KPI tracking or operator guidance. If energy management were assigned to Vehicle, it would require Vehicle to interface with Teleoperation and Safety Services, creating tight coupling and diffusion of energy logic across subsystems.

Teleoperation subsystem owns communication and remote control but does not own propulsion, hydraulics, or safety services. Assigning energy management to Teleoperation would create high diffusion and inappropriate coupling of energy logic with communication protocols.

Safety Services owns safety monitoring and intervention but energy efficiency is not a safety-critical function (though safety must constrain energy optimization). Assigning energy management to Safety Services would violate separation of concerns and create diffusion of non-safety logic into safety-critical module.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Energy management is a system-level cross-cutting concern that integrates data and logic from multiple subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). It is most naturally contained in the NGHE Solution module, which owns system-level integration, KPI tracking, and operator interface. This placement minimizes diffusion and maintains clear separation of concerns: Vehicle owns propulsion/hydraulic efficiency; Teleoperation owns communication efficiency; Safety Services owns safety constraints; NGHE Solution owns energy KPI and guidance.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution module may become a 'god object' if too many cross-cutting concerns are assigned to it. Mitigation: establish clear boundaries for NGHE Solution responsibilities (energy KPI, integration, operator guidance) and resist scope creep. Delegate subsystem-level efficiency optimization to Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services.

Risk: Energy management logic may require real-time performance (low-latency feedback) that conflicts with system-level integration responsibilities. Mitigation: separate energy monitoring (real-time, in Vehicle or edge) from energy guidance (system-level, in NGHE Solution). Use asynchronous telemetry updates.

Risk: Operator guidance may be perceived as intrusive or may conflict with operator preferences. Mitigation: design guidance as advisory only; provide operator override capability; validate via field trials and stakeholder feedback.

Risk: Energy KPI measurement depends on accurate telemetry from multiple subsystems. If telemetry is unreliable, KPI is invalid. Mitigation: implement telemetry validation and redundancy; establish data quality requirements.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish a clear interface contract between NGHE Solution and subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) for energy telemetry. Define telemetry schema, update frequency, accuracy requirements, and failure modes. Separate energy monitoring (real-time, subsystem-level) from energy guidance (system-level, NGHE Solution). Ensure energy guidance is advisory and does not override safety constraints or operator control.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid energy management with system-level KPI tracking, subsystem targets, and real-time feedback) directly addresses FR-NGHE-003 (energy efficiency KPI). The DP is sufficiently specific to guide detailed design (subsystem allocation, telemetry requirements, operator interface) while remaining flexible to accommodate different hardware and operational scenarios. The DP acknowledges coupling between subsystems and provides mechanisms (feedback, guidance) to manage it. Recommended revision: clarify the energy budget allocation algorithm and operator guidance logic in next design phase.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk (Low): Energy telemetry collection is distributed across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems, but aggregation and KPI computation are centralized in NGHE Solution. This is appropriate and minimizes diffusion.

Diffusion Risk (Medium): Operator guidance logic may require integration with Teleoperation subsystem (for remote operator interface) and Vehicle subsystem (for autonomous recommendations). Mitigation: define clear interfaces and keep guidance logic in NGHE Solution; subsystems implement guidance reception and execution.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate container for energy management because it owns system-level integration and operator interface. Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) own subsystem-level efficiency optimization and telemetry provision. This separation of concerns minimizes coupling and diffusion. Recommended revision: establish detailed interface specifications for telemetry and guidance between NGHE Solution and subsystems.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: FR-NGHE-003 is verifiable via measurement of energy_per_ton over the agreed evaluation period. Verification requires: (1) accurate telemetry from Vehicle (fuel consumption, distance, load); (2) operational data from Site Manager (task definitions, evaluation period); (3) KPI computation in NGHE Solution. Feasibility is high if telemetry infrastructure is available. Recommended: establish telemetry requirements and validation procedures in detailed design.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-003 is allocated to NGHE Solution as the system-level KPI owner. Sub-FRs (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-005) are allocated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, and NGHE Solution respectively. Allocation is clear and traceable. Recommended: establish detailed allocation matrix and interface specifications in next design phase.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-C to specify the energy budget allocation algorithm (e.g., proportional to subsystem power consumption, based on duty cycle analysis, or empirical optimization). Establish contingency targets if primary targets are infeasible.

Define the operator guidance logic: what metrics are displayed, what recommendations are provided, how are conflicts between energy optimization and other objectives (productivity, safety, comfort) resolved?
Establish telemetry requirements: sensor types, accuracy, update frequency, failure modes, validation procedures. Define data quality metrics and acceptance criteria.
Separate energy monitoring (real-time, subsystem-level) from energy guidance (system-level, NGHE Solution). Define the interface and data flow between monitoring and guidance.

Establish failure modes and fallback behavior: if energy monitoring fails, system shall revert to safe default operation without energy optimization. Ensure energy guidance does not override safety constraints.
Conduct feasibility study to validate that subsystem-level efficiency targets are achievable without major redesign. Establish contingency targets if needed.
Plan field trials to validate operator response to energy feedback and guidance. Measure actual energy_per_ton improvement and operator satisfaction.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-003 is a system-level KPI requirement that has been successfully refined into a coherent DP (hybrid energy management) and assigned to an appropriate IM (NGHE Solution). The FR is not atomic—it depends on subsystem-level efficiency improvements (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-005) and operational factors (operator behavior, task definition). Decomposition to the next level is necessary to allocate responsibility and establish measurable sub-requirements. The FR-DP-IM gates all pass, indicating readiness to decompose. Recommended: proceed to detailed decomposition of sub-FRs (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-005) in parallel, with integration points at NGHE Solution level.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR is clear and verifiable. The selected DP is sound and addresses the key challenge (integrating energy management across subsystems while maintaining operator control and safety). The assigned IM (NGHE Solution) is appropriate and minimizes diffusion. However, the requirement is inherently multi-level: system-level KPI tracking (NGHE Solution) depends on subsystem-level efficiency (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and operational optimization (operator behavior, task planning). Decomposition is necessary to establish clear ownership, measurable targets, and verification criteria at each level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The propulsion subsystem shall minimize energy consumption per unit distance traveled while maintaining required tractive effort and speed.
FR-EE-002: The hydraulic subsystem shall minimize energy losses in power transmission and actuation while maintaining required force and flow rates.
FR-EE-003: The teleoperation subsystem shall minimize energy overhead (communication, processing, latency compensation) relative to autonomous or direct-control modes.
FR-EE-004: The safety services subsystem shall minimize energy overhead for monitoring, alerting, and intervention while maintaining required safety integrity.
FR-EE-005: The system shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility and efficiency metrics to operators and site managers to enable operational optimization.

assumptions: Telemetry infrastructure (fuel flow sensors, power meters, GPS, CAN bus) is available and accurate to ±5% across all operating conditions.
Operators will respond to real-time efficiency feedback and guidance.
Subsystem-level efficiency targets are achievable without major redesign.
Energy overhead of teleoperation and safety services is quantifiable and separable.
Energy management is advisory and does not override operator control or safety constraints.
Evaluation period and E_target are defined by customer and remain stable during design and deployment.
open_risks: High: Safety criticality of energy management—if energy monitoring or guidance fails, operator may lose situational awareness or make unsafe decisions. Mitigation: ensure energy feedback is advisory only; maintain independent safety monitoring.
Medium: Operator behavior may not change if guidance conflicts with productivity or comfort. Mitigation: design guidance to align with operator incentives; validate via field trials.

Medium: Subsystem-level efficiency targets may be infeasible given current hardware or operational constraints. Mitigation: conduct feasibility study during detailed design; establish contingency targets.
Medium: Energy overhead of teleoperation and safety services may be non-linear or context-dependent. Mitigation: characterize overhead empirically across representative scenarios.
Low: NGHE Solution module may become a 'god object' if too many cross-cutting concerns are assigned to it. Mitigation: establish clear boundaries and resist scope creep.
evidence_needed: Telemetry infrastructure specification and validation plan.
Energy budget allocation algorithm and subsystem-level efficiency targets.
Operator guidance logic specification and user interface design.
Feasibility study results for subsystem-level efficiency improvements.
Field trial plan and success criteria for energy_per_ton improvement.
Interface specifications between NGHE Solution and subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services).
Failure mode analysis and fallback behavior specification for energy management.
Data quality requirements and telemetry validation procedures.
